metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: 20th Century World History 12 examines the most transformative century in modern history, exploring how nationalism, imperialism, technology, and ideology reshaped the world. Students investigate the causes and consequences of the World Wars, the Cold War, decolonization, human rights movements, and cultural change. The course uses historical inquiry to help students assess competing accounts, evaluate source reliability, and make reasoned ethical judgments about controversial events.
metadata.general_description: A rigorous study of world history from 1900 to 2000, examining nationalism, global wars, ideological conflicts, decolonization, human rights movements, and technological change through historical inquiry.

big_ideas.statement: Nationalist movements can unite people in common causes or lead to intense conflict between different groups.
big_ideas.elaboration: Nationalism fuelled both liberation movements (decolonization, independence) and devastating conflicts (WWI, WWII, ethnic cleansing) across the 20th century.

big_ideas.statement: The rapid development and proliferation of technology in the 20th century led to profound social, economic, and political changes.
big_ideas.elaboration: From the machine gun to the atom bomb, from radio to the internet, technological change accelerated the pace of history and transformed everyday life.

big_ideas.statement: The breakdown of long-standing empires created new economic and political systems.
big_ideas.elaboration: The collapse of the Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, British, and Soviet empires reshaped the global map, creating new nations and new conflicts.

categories.name: Use historical inquiry processes and skills
categories.raw: Use historical inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; gather, interpret, and analyze ideas; and communicate findings and decisions.

competencies.raw: Use historical inquiry to ask questions, gather, interpret, and analyze ideas, and communicate findings.
i_can_statements: I can formulate meaningful historical inquiry questions.
I can gather and evaluate historical sources to build a reasoned argument.
I can present my historical findings in a well-structured and evidence-based format.
I can revise my interpretations when confronted with new evidence.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Assess the significance of people, locations, events, and developments, and compare varying perspectives on their historical significance.

competencies.raw: Assess the significance of people, locations, events, and developments in 20th century world history.
i_can_statements: I can apply criteria (scale, duration, depth of change) to evaluate historical significance.
I can compare how the same event is viewed as significant by different nations or groups.
I can argue for a ranking of the most significant events of the 20th century with evidence.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Assess the justification for competing historical accounts after investigating points of contention, reliability of sources, and adequacy of evidence.

competencies.raw: Assess the justification for competing historical accounts by investigating source reliability and adequacy of evidence.
i_can_statements: I can compare two competing historical accounts and evaluate which is more credible.
I can identify bias, perspective, and motive in primary and secondary sources.
I can explain what evidence is sufficient to support a historical claim.
I can analyze propaganda as a historical source.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes for different groups at particular times and places.

competencies.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes for different groups at particular times and places.
i_can_statements: I can trace how the role of women changed across the 20th century in different countries.
I can identify what stayed constant in international relations even as specific events changed.
I can compare how different nations experienced the same global event (e.g., WWII, the Cold War).

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Assess how underlying conditions and the actions of individuals or groups affect events, decisions, and developments.

competencies.raw: Assess how underlying conditions and the actions of individuals or groups affect events, decisions, and developments.
i_can_statements: I can identify the MAIN (Militarism, Alliances, Imperialism, Nationalism) causes of WWI.
I can evaluate how one leader's decisions (e.g., Hitler, Stalin, Churchill) changed the course of history.
I can trace the long-term consequences of events like the Treaty of Versailles or the dropping of atomic bombs.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain different perspectives on past or present people, places, issues, and events by considering prevailing norms, values, worldviews, and beliefs.

competencies.raw: Explain different perspectives on past or present people, places, issues, and events.
i_can_statements: I can explain how the same Cold War event looked different from American, Soviet, and non-aligned perspectives.
I can analyze how colonized peoples experienced decolonization differently from European powers.
I can avoid imposing present-day values anachronistically on historical figures.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about controversial actions in the past or present, and assess whether we have a responsibility to respond.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about controversial actions in the past or present.
i_can_statements: I can make an ethical judgment about the decision to use atomic weapons, considering context and consequences.
I can evaluate the responsibility of bystanders and non-combatants during genocide.
I can argue whether modern nations have a responsibility to respond to historical injustices committed by their predecessors.

areas.name: Authoritarian Regimes
areas.raw: Authoritarian regimes including examples from Chile, Cambodia, Cuba, Soviet Union, North Korea, China, Germany, and Italy.
topics: The rise of fascism: Mussolini's Italy, Hitler's Germany
Stalinist Soviet Union: collectivization, the gulags, purges
Mao's China: the Great Leap Forward, Cultural Revolution
Pol Pot and the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia
Authoritarian regimes in Latin America: Chile under Pinochet, Cuba under Castro
North Korea: the Kim dynasty and totalitarianism
Comparing authoritarian ideologies: fascism, communism, military dictatorship

areas.name: Civil Wars, Independence Movements, and Revolutions
areas.raw: Civil wars, independence movements, and revolutions including the Soviet Union 1917-21, Chinese revolution, decolonization, Iranian Revolution, and Vietnam War.
topics: The Russian Revolution (1917): February and October revolutions
The Russian Civil War and the formation of the Soviet Union
The Chinese Civil War and the founding of the People's Republic of China
Decolonization in Africa and Asia: independence movements
The Iranian Revolution (1979)
The Vietnam War: causes, course, and consequences
Post-colonial nation-building and its challenges

areas.name: Human Rights Movements
areas.raw: Human rights movements including women's equality, US civil rights, anti-apartheid, and Latin American workers' movements.
topics: The women's suffrage movement and second-wave feminism
The American civil rights movement: key figures, events, and legislation
The anti-apartheid movement in South Africa: Mandela, the ANC, international pressure
Labour and workers' rights movements in Latin America
The LGBTQ+ rights movement across the 20th century
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948)

areas.name: Religious, Ethnic, and Cultural Conflicts
areas.raw: Religious, ethnic, and cultural conflicts including Indigenous cultural genocide, Armenian genocide, Holocaust, Cambodia, Rwanda, separatist movements.
topics: The Armenian Genocide (1915–1923)
The Holocaust: causes, implementation, and legacy
Cultural genocide against Indigenous peoples globally
The Cambodian Genocide under the Khmer Rouge
The Rwandan Genocide (1994)
Ethnic and religious separatist movements (e.g., Yugoslavia, Northern Ireland)
Genocide denial movements and their political use

areas.name: Global Conflicts
areas.raw: Global conflicts including WWI, WWII, the Cold War, the arms race, militarism, espionage, and military technology evolution.
topics: WWI: causes (MAIN), key battles, trench warfare, and outcomes
WWII: causes, major theatres, the Holocaust, atomic bombs, outcomes
The Cold War: origins, proxy wars, arms race, and détente
The Korean War and its legacy
Nuclear deterrence and MAD (Mutually Assured Destruction)
Military technology: tanks, aircraft, missiles, nuclear weapons
Espionage and intelligence in the Cold War

areas.name: Migrations and Territorial Boundaries
areas.raw: Migrations and territorial boundaries including post-WWI Middle East, Palestine, suburbanization.
topics: Post-WWI redrawn borders: the Sykes-Picot Agreement, Mandate Palestine
The creation of Israel and the Palestinian conflict
Refugee crises of the 20th century
The partition of India (1947) and mass migration
Post-WWII European migration and displaced persons
Suburbanization and internal migration in North America

areas.name: International Cooperation
areas.raw: International cooperation including UN peacekeeping missions.
topics: The League of Nations: structure, aims, and failures
The United Nations: founding, structure, and peacekeeping
International law and war crimes tribunals (Nuremberg, ICTY, ICC)
Canada's role in UN peacekeeping
NATO and collective security
International humanitarian organizations: Red Cross, UNHCR

areas.name: Social and Cultural Developments
areas.raw: Social and cultural developments including women's roles, consumerism, and globalization trends.
topics: The changing role of women in 20th century society
The rise of consumer culture: advertising, mass production, the 'American Dream'
Globalization: cultural exchange, cultural imperialism, and resistance
Youth culture: the 1960s counterculture, music, and social change
Immigration and multiculturalism in Western nations

areas.name: Communication and Transportation Technologies
areas.raw: Communication and transportation technologies including propaganda, the automobile, media influence, and television and radio effects.
topics: The role of radio in 20th century politics (FDR, Nazi Germany)
Television and political communication
Propaganda as a political tool: posters, film, radio
The automobile and its social, economic, and environmental impact
The space race and technological competition
The rise of the internet and digital communication
